questions.question: The term National Income represents:
questions.option_1: Gross national product at market prices minus depreciation
questions.option_2: Gross National product at market prices minus depreciation plus net factor income from abroad
questions.option_3: Gross national product at market prices minus depreciation and indirect taxes plus subsidies
questions.option_4: Gross national product at market prices minus net factor income from abroad
questions.solution:
Structural Derivation of National Income

In national income accounting, formal National Income specifically refers to Net National Product (NNP) at Factor Cost. It represents the total income earned by the factors of production belonging to a country.

The structural conversion from Gross National Product (GNP) at Market Prices to Net National Product at Factor Cost is mathematically derived using the following steps:

  1. First, we deduct depreciation from GNP to convert the metric from "Gross" to "Net":
    NNP at Market Prices=GNP at Market Prices-Depreciation
  2. Next, to transition from "Market Prices" (the price paid by consumers) to "Factor Cost" (the actual cost of production), we must remove the distorting effects of government intervention by subtracting indirect taxes and adding back any subsidies received:
    NNP at Factor Cost (National Income)=NNP at Market Prices-Indirect Taxes+Subsidies

Combining these two operations gives the exact definition: Gross National Product at market prices minus depreciation and indirect taxes plus subsidies.

questions.question: Gilt-edged market means
questions.solution:
Defining the Gilt-Edged Market

The term gilt-edged market refers explicitly to the market for the buying, selling, and trading of Government Securities (G-Secs) and treasury bills.

Key features of this financial market include:

  • Zero Default Risk: These securities are backed by the sovereign guarantee of the government. Because the risk of default is virtually non-existent, they are considered ultra-safe investments.
  • Origin of the Term: The phrase originates from Great Britain, where the British government traditionally issued its formal bond certificates with gilded or gold-painted edges to signify their high status and unmatched security.
  • Institutional Framework: In India, the market is primarily institutional and is strictly regulated and managed by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI).

questions.question: "... instil into the vast millions of workers, men and women, who actually do the job, a sense of partnership and of cooperative performance" The above passage related to
questions.solution:
Historical Context of the Community Development Programme

The quote highlighting the importance of instilling "a sense of partnership and of cooperative performance" among millions of workers is directly associated with the philosophy of the Community Development Programme (CDP).

  • Launch and Objective: Launched in India on October 2, 1952, the CDP was one of the earliest and largest comprehensive rural reconstruction initiatives implemented post-independence.
  • Core Philosophy: It aimed at transforming the socio-economic life of rural communities through self-help and local cooperative effort. The core intent was to move away from top-down bureaucratic mandates and instead build a sense of active democratic partnership among ordinary citizens and village workers to build rural infrastructure, health, and agriculture.

questions.question: The growth rate of per capita income at current prices is higher than that of per capita income at constant prices because the latter takes into account the rate of
questions.solution:
Nominal vs. Real Per Capita Income Metrics

The variation in growth rates between per capita income evaluated at current prices versus constant prices comes down to how inflation is handled:

  • Current Prices (Nominal Per Capita Income): Measures the average income per person using the prevailing market prices of the current year. It reflects both real increases in output and any changes in the price level (inflation).
  • Constant Prices (Real Per Capita Income): Measures the income using prices from a fixed base year. By holding prices completely fixed, it strips out the distortions of market inflation.

Conclusion: The growth rate of per capita income at current prices is always higher during inflationary periods because it includes price inflation. The calculation at constant prices scales down this nominal figure because it specifically adjusts for the **increase in price level** over time.

questions.question: The supply-side economics lays greater emphasis on the point of view of
questions.solution:
Core Principles of Supply-Side Economics

Supply-side economics is a macroeconomic school of thought that emerged prominently in the late 1970s, starkly contrasting with traditional Keynesian demand-management frameworks.

  • Primary Emphasis: This theory places its fundamental focus on the **Producer** (businesses, entrepreneurs, and investors) rather than the consumer.
  • Underlying Logic: It argues that the most effective way to drive long-term economic growth is by increasing the aggregate supply of goods and services. This is achieved by cutting marginal income and corporate tax rates, reducing regulatory compliance burdens, and encouraging free-market capital investments.
  • The Incentive Mechanism: By leaving more profit in the hands of producers, they are heavily incentivized to expand factories, innovate, and hire workers. This concept is often popularized through the framework of the Laffer Curve.

questions.question: A consumer is said to be in equilibrium, if
questions.option_1: He is able to fulfil his need with a given level of income
questions.option_2: He is able to live in full comforts with a given level of income
questions.option_3: He can fulfil his needs without consumption of certain items
questions.option_4: He is able to locate new sources of income
questions.solution:
Microeconomic Criteria for Consumer Equilibrium

In microeconomic consumer theory, a consumer is defined to be in a state of **equilibrium** when they have maximized their total utility (satisfaction) given their budget constraints.

  • Operational Definition: A consumer reaches this state when **he is able to fulfill his needs with a given level of income** in such a manner that there is absolutely no incentive to alter his spending pattern.
  • Mathematical Condition: This balance is struck when the marginal utility per rupee spent is equal across all purchased commodities, satisfying the condition:
    MUxPx=MUyPy=MUm
    Where MU represents marginal utility and P represents the market price. At this junction, the consumer has optimally allocated their limited financial resources to match their subjective preferences perfectly.
